selectedHook.name: useCurrentMatches (createEffect pattern)
selectedHook.rationale: Four hooks are available. useBeforeLeave fires before navigation completes and is explicitly documented as unreliable on browser back/forward — it fails the 'afterCancellation' and 'afterRedirects' requirements. useIsRouting returns a boolean signal only (true/false during transition) — it provides no route pattern information, so it cannot produce a view name. useLocation passes afterCancellation and afterRedirects and provides location.pathname, but only the evaluated URL, not the route template — view-name reconstruction from a live URL requires param substitution (least-preferred family). useCurrentMatches also passes afterCancellation and afterRedirects, and additionally exposes m.route.path (the route pattern e.g. /users/:id) for every match in the nested route tree — this enables the matched-records view-name algorithm with no guesswork. useCurrentMatches is therefore the clear winner.

wrappingStrategy.target: A SolidJS component (e.g. <DatadogRouterTracker />) that calls useCurrentMatches() and useLocation() inside a createEffect(), placed as a child of the application's Router (or inside the root layout component).
wrappingStrategy.rationale: SolidJS Router has no imperative router factory with a subscribe() API equivalent to React Router's createBrowserRouter. All routing primitives (useCurrentMatches, useLocation) are reactive signals that must be consumed inside the reactive ownership tree — i.e., inside a component or createRoot. A renderless component is therefore the idiomatic SolidJS pattern: the user drops <DatadogRouterTracker /> inside their Router or root layout, and our createEffect() re-runs reactively whenever location.pathname changes. This is consistent with the canonical SolidJS analytics integration pattern documented in the SolidJS community (createEffect over location.pathname inside a layout).

viewNameAlgorithm.rationale: useCurrentMatches returns an array of RouteMatch objects, each exposing m.route.path — the parameterized route pattern for that nested segment (e.g. /users/:id). Iterating and concatenating these segments (handling both absolute and relative paths, exactly as the React and Vue integrations do) produces a clean, PII-safe view name. Catch-all segments (/* and /*rest) should be substituted with the actual path remainder from location.pathname, mirroring the React integration's substitutePathSplats and the Vue integration's substituteCatchAll. Optional segments (:id?) are preserved in the view name as-is (e.g. /stories/:id?), which is correct and informative.

referenceImplementation.rationale: rum-vue is the closest structural match: it also wraps a framework-specific router factory function (createRouter from vue-router) and uses an afterEach hook to call startVueRouterView with matched route records. The SolidJS integration differs in that it uses a renderless component + createEffect instead of a factory wrapper, but the overall package shape (plugin, startRouterView, computeViewName, entries/main + entries/router), the plugin API (solidjsPlugin({ router: true }), onRumInit, trackViewsManually), and the computeViewName algorithm (iterate matched records, handle absolute/relative paths, substitute catch-alls) are all directly analogous. rum-react is the secondary reference for the matched-records view-name logic (substitutePathSplats).

ssr.handling: Guard the createEffect body with a check for the client environment. SolidJS provides isServer from 'solid-js/web' (true during SSR). The renderless component's createEffect should return early when isServer is true, so no view tracking fires on the server. Alternatively, since createEffect does not run during SSR in SolidJS (effects are client-only by design), no explicit guard may be needed — but an explicit isServer check is recommended for clarity and future-proofing.

notes:
1. WRAPPING API: The user places <DatadogSolidRouterTracker /> inside their Router tree (e.g. as a sibling to their routes inside the root layout). The package exports this component from '@datadog/browser-rum-solidjs/solid-router'. The SolidJS plugin (solidjsPlugin) is passed to datadogRum.init({ plugins: [...] }) with { router: true } to enable manual view tracking.

2. CATCH-ALL SUBSTITUTION: SolidJS Router uses /* (bare) or /*rest (named) for catch-all routes. The named catch-all rest value is accessible via useParams().rest but is NOT available from useCurrentMatches — m.route.path gives /*rest as the pattern. For catch-all substitution we use location.pathname (from useLocation) to reconstruct the real path, mirroring the React and Vue approaches.

3. OPTIONAL SEGMENTS: Routes like /stories/:id? appear verbatim in m.route.path. The view name preserves the ? suffix as-is, giving /stories/:id? for both /stories and /stories/123.

4. HASH AND MEMORY ROUTERS: SolidJS provides <HashRouter> and <MemoryRouter> variants. The renderless component pattern works identically for all three since it relies on useCurrentMatches/useLocation signals, not on the router type.

5. FILE-BASED ROUTING (SolidStart): File-based routes via <FileRoutes /> also populate useCurrentMatches correctly, so no special handling is needed.

6. VERSIONS: The v0.10+ API (no <Outlet>, no <Routes> wrapper, no useRoutes) is the target. The package should declare @solidjs/router >=0.10.0 as a peer dependency.

7. DEDUPLICATION: The createEffect fires whenever location.pathname changes. This naturally deduplicates — if only the query string or hash changes, location.pathname stays the same and no new view is started, consistent with SDK automatic view tracking behaviour.
